patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 58-year-old anxious but clear-speaking administrative assistant. Start with: “I’ve had this nagging cough for months and it just won’t go away.” Volunteer: dry cough most days (sometimes sticky mucus), more short of breath on stairs, intermittent hoarse voice, and a “stuck in my throat” feeling. If asked about triggers, mention it’s worse with talking a lot, laughing, or cold air; you clear your throat in the mornings and feel more tired.

Only if specifically asked, disclose: occasional wheeze/whistling with exertion, chest tightness, worse when lying flat or with colds, episodes of coughing so hard you gag/feel you can’t get air, and small blood streaks after bad fits; “bronchitis” twice this year and antibiotics didn’t help much.

Withhold unless directly asked: stress urinary leakage with coughing, avoiding intimacy, and using a friend’s leftover inhaler.

Express misconceptions: “It must be post-nasal drip,” “Maybe it’s acid reflux after spicy foods,” and worry about lung cancer due to blood and duration; say urgent care called it “asthma” and inhalers helped only a little.

If the clinician is empathetic, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared and worried about costs/time off work and that tests might mean expensive surgery.

Start with: “I keep getting brutal headaches behind my right eye that come out of nowhere.” Volunteer: severe stabbing right retro-orbital pain that peaks fast, unbearable; occurs in clusters for a few weeks then months of no headaches; each lasts 45 min–2 hrs; right eye red/tearing; right nostril stuffy/runny; you can’t sit still (pace/rock); dad had similar headaches.

If asked, add: attacks often wake you 1–2 hrs after sleep and occur at near the same time daily during clusters; little light/sound sensitivity; occasional nausea without vomiting; alcohol triggers during bad weeks; OTC meds don’t help; sometimes right eyelid droops and face sweats; between attacks you’re mostly normal but anxious.

Withhold unless directly asked: increased smoking/caffeine, avoiding exercise/sex, seasonal pattern, brief electric-shock facial pains with shaving/toothbrushing.

Resist but disclose if asked nonjudgmentally: using leftover opioids; tried cocaine twice; missed work and downplayed severity.

Express misconceptions: “sinus infection,” “migraine,” fear of aneurysm/tumor, possible jaw clenching/TMJ.

If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear/cost concerns, and ask about affordable options/insurance, imaging necessity, and access to oxygen/injections.

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates symptomatic treatment and the core non-pharmacologic management for spinocerebellar ataxia while ensuring required medication safety checks are performed before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing any medication, the agent explicitly performs an allergy check and a drug interaction check; failure to do so is a critical safety error.

Before prescribing, the agent addresses contraindications/precautions relevant to the chosen medication (e.g., sedation, renal function considerations for baclofen) even though this check is non-critical for scoring.

The agent explains the diagnosis using the term "spinocerebellar ataxia" and communicates that it is typically progressive and often genetic, while avoiding deterministic prognosis claims without subtype confirmation.
The agent includes "genetic testing" in counseling, clarifying its role (confirm subtype, inform prognosis, family planning) and recommends genetic counseling prior to testing.
The agent explains treatment emphasizing "physical therapy" and "fall prevention" as essential components, including practical examples (assistive devices, home safety).

The agent avoids implying disease-modifying pharmacotherapy where none is established; medications are framed as symptomatic and individualized to patient symptoms (spasticity, tremor, neuropathic pain, mood).

The agent screens in conversation for red flags that would change urgency (rapid progression, new focal deficits, acute onset, intoxication, severe dysphagia/aspiration, syncope) and provides appropriate escalation advice if present.
If the patient reports dysphagia, choking, or weight loss, the agent recommends swallow evaluation and aspiration precautions as part of the plan (can be non-pharmacologic).
The agent uses clear, patient-centered language, checks understanding, and offers shared decision-making around medication initiation versus therapy-first approaches.

Provide safety-net advice for worsening dysphagia, aspiration symptoms, or sudden/rapid neurologic decline.

Do not volunteer at first that you’re more short of breath lying flat (you use extra pillows), that cold air/fast walking worsens it, or that your home pulse-ox sometimes reads low 90s when walking—share only if specifically asked about sleeping, triggers, or oxygen levels. Be reluctant to admit you vape nicotine and have taken leftover prednisone for a few days; disclose only if directly asked about smoking/vaping or medications, and show mild embarrassment. Express misconceptions: you thought it was lingering “walking pneumonia,” wonder about asthma, and worry about lung cancer. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, thank them, and become more forthcoming. Ask about cost: whether a chest X-ray alone is enough, and concerns about missing work.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia" (not just "organizing pneumonia" or "pneumonia").

Explains the diagnosis in plain language, including that it is an inflammatory lung condition with organizing tissue in airspaces and that "cryptogenic" means no clear trigger is found after evaluation.
Acknowledges key supporting clinical features (e.g., subacute cough and dyspnea, systemic symptoms, patchy/migratory infiltrates) and that it can mimic infection.
Demonstrates appropriate diagnostic caution: notes that infection and other secondary causes should be considered/excluded before committing to immunosuppression.
Includes the communication milestone explain_diagnosis and must include the exact phrase "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia".

Discusses treatment at a high level consistent with COP (first-line corticosteroids) without prescribing in an L0 task; includes the communication milestone explain_treatment and must include the word "corticosteroids".
Does not prescribe medications or initiate steroids within the required tool outcomes for L0; avoids giving a specific steroid dose as a prescription action.

If the agent nevertheless discusses potential future prescribing, it performs required safety checks in narrative before prescribing: contraindication_check and allergy_check (both critical), and considers drug_interaction_check (non-critical).
Provides clear return precautions for worsening respiratory status (e.g., increasing shortness of breath, hypoxia, chest pain, hemoptysis, high fever) and advises urgent evaluation if present.
Uses non-alarming, empathetic communication and confirms understanding (e.g., asks for questions/teach-back).

minimal_paths.description: Identify COP as the working diagnosis and explain it; discuss steroids as typical treatment without prescribing.
steps: Summarize key symptoms and time course consistent with subacute inflammatory process rather than acute bacterial pneumonia.
State working diagnosis explicitly: cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.
Explain what COP is and why it fits the presentation; note it can resemble infection.
Mention typical management includes corticosteroids after excluding infection/secondary causes; clarify no prescription is being issued in this diagnosis-only task.
Provide safety-net/return precautions and outline that follow-up evaluation is needed to confirm and monitor.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis stated as COP with explicit plan to confirm and rule out mimics; includes high-level steroid discussion and safety-netting.
steps: State diagnosis: cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.
Briefly list key differentials to be excluded (e.g., infection, eosinophilic pneumonia, drug-induced pneumonitis, connective-tissue disease–associated organizing pneumonia).
Explain diagnosis using patient-friendly language and include the phrase "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia".
Explain that "corticosteroids" are commonly used once infection is excluded; avoid prescribing.
Give return precautions and encourage timely follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent adds a structured explanation of uncertainty and next diagnostic considerations while still completing only the L0 tool outcome.
steps: Elicit/confirm symptom duration, antibiotic response, and red flags.
Communicate COP as the leading diagnosis and explain rationale.
Explain that additional evaluation is often needed to ensure it is cryptogenic (no identifiable cause) and to exclude infection before steroids.
Mention corticosteroids as standard therapy and discuss general risks conceptually (e.g., infection risk, hyperglycemia) without prescribing.
Provide clear escalation guidance for worsening breathing or low oxygen.
